title: Control and Coordination
summary: Chapter 6 covers how organisms control and coordinate their body functions through the nervous system and hormones. The human nervous system consists of the brain, spinal cord, and nerves. Reflex actions, reflex arc, voluntary and involuntary actions are explained. Plant movements in response to stimuli (tropisms and nastic movements) and plant hormones are studied. Human endocrine glands and their hormones are covered.
learningPoints: Define nervous system and its components: neurons, brain, spinal cord
Distinguish voluntary, involuntary, and reflex actions
Describe the reflex arc: receptor → sensory neuron → relay neuron → motor neuron → effector
Draw a labelled diagram of a neuron
Explain how nerve impulse is transmitted across a synapse
Describe the functions of different brain parts: cerebrum, cerebellum, medulla, pons
Explain tropisms in plants: phototropism, geotropism, hydrotropism, thigmotropism
Explain role of plant hormones: auxin, gibberellin, cytokinin, abscisic acid
Name human endocrine glands and their hormones
State functions of insulin, adrenaline, thyroxine, testosterone, estrogen, growth hormone

laws.name: All-or-Nothing Law of Nerve Impulse
laws.statement: A nerve fibre either transmits a full impulse or none at all. The strength of impulse does not vary — only frequency varies.

formulas.label: Reflex Arc
formulas.expression: Receptor → Sensory neuron → Relay neuron → Motor neuron → Effector
formulas.description: Path of nerve impulse in a reflex action. Bypasses brain for speed.

diagrams.title: Structure of a Neuron
diagrams.description: Cell body (cyton), dendrites (receive impulse), axon (long fibre, conducts impulse), myelin sheath, axon terminal (synaptic knob). Synapse between two neurons.

diagrams.title: Reflex Arc Diagram
diagrams.description: Receptor (e.g. skin) → Sensory neuron → Spinal cord (relay neuron) → Motor neuron → Effector (muscle). Shows how reflex bypasses brain.

diagrams.title: Human Brain — Schematic
diagrams.description: Cerebrum (thinking, memory), Cerebellum (balance, coordination), Medulla oblongata (breathing, heart rate), Pons (relay signals), Hypothalamus (body temperature, hunger).

resources.label: Karnataka SSLC Science Textbook
resources.description: Official Karnataka SSLC Science textbook. Chapter 6 covers Control and Coordination.

resources.label: NCERT Science Textbook Class 10
resources.description: NCERT Class 10 Science Chapter 7 Control and Coordination.

resources.label: Control and Coordination — Video Lessons
resources.description: Khan Academy videos on nervous system, reflex arc, brain and hormones.

pyqs.question: The name of part X (synapse) and its function is: synapse; transmits chemical signals generated at axon terminal to dendrite.
pyqs.answer: Synapse: The junction between two neurons. Neurotransmitters are released from axon terminal and bind to receptors on dendrite, transmitting the impulse chemically across the gap.

pyqs.question: Why does the stem of a growing plant bend towards light? Explain.
pyqs.answer: When light comes from one side, auxin (plant hormone) synthesised at shoot tip diffuses to the shady side. Higher auxin concentration on shady side causes faster cell elongation. The stem bends toward the light source. This is phototropism.

pyqs.question: Name the hormones: (i) Controlling sugar level in blood (ii) Preparing body for emergency (iii) Controlling fats, proteins and carbohydrates metabolism
pyqs.answer: (i) Insulin (secreted by pancreas, lowers blood glucose) (ii) Adrenaline/Epinephrine (adrenal gland, fight-or-flight) (iii) Thyroxine (thyroid gland, regulates metabolism)

pyqs.question: Name the prominent parts of reflex arc.
pyqs.answer: Receptor, sensory neuron, relay neuron (in spinal cord), motor neuron, effector (muscle/gland).

pyqs.question: What is the function of abscisic acid in plants?
pyqs.answer: Abscisic acid (ABA) inhibits plant growth. It promotes seed dormancy, leaf fall (abscission), and stomata closure during water stress.

pyqs.question: Which hormones are responsible for sexual maturation in humans?
pyqs.answer: In males: Testosterone (secreted by testes). In females: Estrogen (secreted by ovaries). Both are sex hormones responsible for secondary sexual characteristics at puberty.

mcqs.question: The synapse transmits:
options: B. Chemical signals as electrical impulses
C. Chemical signals from axon terminal to dendrite
mcqs.explanation: At synapse: neurotransmitters released from axon terminal cross the synaptic gap and bind to dendrite receptors, transmitting impulse chemically.

mcqs.question: Hormone that controls sugar level in blood:
mcqs.explanation: Insulin (from β-cells of islets of Langerhans in pancreas) lowers blood glucose by promoting glucose uptake by cells.

mcqs.question: Which part of the brain controls balance and coordination?
mcqs.explanation: Cerebellum maintains posture, balance and coordination of muscle movements. Cerebrum: thinking, memory.

mcqs.question: Abscisic acid in plants:
mcqs.explanation: ABA (abscisic acid) is a growth inhibitor. Promotes dormancy, leaf fall, and stomata closure in drought.

mcqs.question: Hormones responsible for sexual maturation in males:
mcqs.explanation: Testosterone secreted by testes causes development of secondary sexual characters in males at puberty.

mcqs.question: Parts of the reflex arc in correct order:
options: A. Effector → Motor neuron → Relay → Sensory → Receptor
B. Receptor → Sensory → Relay → Motor → Effector
C. Receptor → Motor → Relay → Sensory → Effector
D. Brain → Sensory → Receptor → Motor → Effector
mcqs.explanation: Reflex arc: Receptor (detects stimulus) → Sensory neuron (to spinal cord) → Relay neuron → Motor neuron → Effector (muscle/gland).

mcqs.question: Why does the stem bend toward light?
options: A. Auxin accumulates on illuminated side
B. Auxin accumulates on shady side causing elongation
mcqs.explanation: Auxin moves to shady side → more elongation on that side → stem bends toward light source. This is phototropism.

mcqs.question: Adrenaline is secreted by:
mcqs.explanation: Adrenal gland (on top of kidneys) secretes adrenaline. It prepares body for fight or flight — increases heart rate, dilates pupils, increases blood glucose.

mcqs.question: Iodine deficiency causes:
mcqs.explanation: Thyroxine requires iodine. Iodine deficiency → less thyroxine → thyroid gland enlarges (goitre). Iodised salt prevents it.

mcqs.question: Two differences between CNS and PNS:
options: A. CNS has brain+spinal cord; PNS has cranial and spinal nerves
B. CNS has only brain; PNS has spinal cord
C. CNS processes; PNS does not
mcqs.explanation: CNS = Brain + Spinal cord (receives and integrates information). PNS = Cranial nerves + Spinal nerves (connects CNS to body).

mcqs.question: Plant hormone that promotes cell elongation and phototropism:
mcqs.explanation: Auxin (indole acetic acid, IAA) promotes cell elongation. Asymmetric distribution causes tropism movements.

mcqs.question: Thyroxine controls:
options: C. Fats, proteins and carbohydrate metabolism
mcqs.explanation: Thyroxine (thyroid hormone, contains iodine) regulates the metabolism of fats, proteins and carbohydrates in the body.

mcqs.question: Reflex action is controlled by:
mcqs.explanation: Reflex actions are controlled by spinal cord (not brain) — this allows very fast responses without waiting for brain processing.

mcqs.question: Which movement in plants is NOT a tropism?
mcqs.explanation: Tropisms are directional growth responses. Nastic movements (like touch-me-not closing) are non-directional responses to stimuli.

mcqs.question: Insulin is secreted by:
mcqs.explanation: Islets of Langerhans in pancreas secrete insulin (β-cells) and glucagon (α-cells) to regulate blood glucose.

exercises.question: Draw a labelled diagram of a neuron. Explain how impulse is transmitted.
exercises.answer: Dendrites receive impulse → cell body processes it → axon conducts to axon terminal → neurotransmitters released across synapse → bind to next neuron's dendrites. Myelin sheath (Schwann cells) speeds up conduction. Nodes of Ranvier between myelin segments.

exercises.question: Explain with diagram how a reflex arc functions.
exercises.answer: Example: touching hot object. Receptor (skin) detects heat → sensory neuron carries impulse to spinal cord → relay neuron in spinal cord → motor neuron → effector (arm muscle contracts, withdrawing hand). Brain receives signal AFTER reflex occurs. Speed advantage.

exercises.question: Write two differences between CNS and PNS.
exercises.answer: CNS: includes brain and spinal cord; receives and integrates all information; protected by skull and vertebral column. PNS: includes 12 cranial nerves and 31 spinal nerves; connects CNS to sense organs and effectors; facilitates communication between CNS and body.

exercises.question: Name the hormones and glands for: blood glucose control, emergency response, metabolism regulation.
exercises.answer: Blood glucose: Insulin from pancreas (β-cells). Emergency: Adrenaline from adrenal glands. Metabolism: Thyroxine from thyroid gland. Deficiency: insulin → diabetes; iodine/thyroxine → goitre; growth hormone → dwarfism.

exercises.question: Explain phototropism and geotropism with examples.
exercises.answer: Phototropism: growth in response to light. Shoots show positive phototropism (grow toward light). Roots show negative phototropism. Caused by asymmetric auxin distribution. Geotropism: growth in response to gravity. Roots show positive geotropism (grow downward). Shoots show negative geotropism (grow upward).

exercises.question: Distinguish between voluntary and involuntary actions.
exercises.answer: Voluntary actions: conscious, controlled by cerebrum (thinking brain). Examples: writing, walking, speaking. Involuntary actions: not conscious, controlled by medulla/hypothalamus. Examples: heartbeat, breathing, peristalsis. Reflex actions: involuntary but very fast, controlled by spinal cord.

exercises.question: What is the role of auxin in plant growth?
exercises.answer: Auxin (IAA) promotes cell elongation. Synthesised at shoot tip. Moves to shady side → cells elongate more → stem bends toward light (phototropism). In roots: high auxin inhibits growth. Auxin also promotes root growth at low concentrations.

exercises.question: Explain the role of feedback mechanism in hormone regulation.
exercises.answer: Example: When blood glucose rises, pancreas releases insulin which lowers glucose. When glucose returns to normal, insulin secretion stops. This is negative feedback. Similarly, thyroid hormone regulates its own secretion through TSH from pituitary. Maintains homeostasis.
